coredata.dc:title: Development of an accurate synchronous transport signal hand-held sensing platform for fluorescence-based berberine on-site detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Liucheng
Du, Liyue
Zhang, Yan
Gao, Jie
Cui, Fengling
coredata.dc:description:
Background Berberine is widely used in clinical treatment because of its wide antibacterial spectrum and low toxic side effects. However, its abuse could lead to bacterial resistance and several other adverse effects. In addition, measuring the content of berberine in environmental water samples helps to monitor its accumulation and metabolism in ecosystems. Traditional detection methods usually need to be carried out in the laboratory, involving complex processing procedures, which are not only time-consuming, but also unfavorable for rapid response and decision-making. Therefore, it is necessary to develop portable instruments to provide reasonable guidance on the addition and intake of berberine to reduce the harm caused by its abuse.
Results In this work, an accurate synchronous transport signal hand-held sensing platform (STSHSP) with a low-cost, easy-to-manufacture, independent use was developed by using photoelectric conversion technology, Bluetooth technology, remote synchronous signal technology, electrical technology, and 3D printing technology. To verify the performance of STSHSP, a 5-oxo-2,3-dihydro-5H-thiazolo [3,2-a] pyridine-3,7-dicarboxylic acid (TPDCA) with ultra-high quantum yield was designed and synthesized as a probe. TPDCA exhibited bright blue fluorescence under the ultraviolet light of 365 nm which could be quenched by berberine through the inner filter effect. In the range of 0.1–80 μg/mL, the voltage displayed by the prepared STSHSP has a good linearity with the berberine concentration (R2 = 0.9997) with a detection limit of 28.32 ng/mL. The portable sensor demonstrated good stability, accuracy, and reliability in detecting actual river water, urine, traditional Chinese medicine, and its preparation samples.
Significance The sensor with its compact structure, portability, and simple operation was suitable for in-situ detection with accurate, reliable, and feasible results, which is beneficial for improving drug quality and ensuring human health. Fortunately, the device could transmit the information to the control center and/or a third-party supervision institution in real-time, which could effectively eliminate the trust crisis. The sensor has broad application prospects in the field of environmental water quality detection and drug safety.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hand-held sensing platform
Remote data synchronous
Field assay
Fluorimetry
Berberine sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of synchronous transport signal hand-held sensing platform
Design of smartphone information collection and storage app
Synthesis of verification probe for STSHSP performance
Determination of berberine based on fluorophotometer
Detection of berberine in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: External dimensions and weight of STSHSP (a). Schematic diagram of the STSHSP design concept (b). Schematic diagram of STSHSP internal structure (c).

The FLV-Test app interface, login interface, interface without STSHSP, interface connecting STSHSP, and working interface (from left to right) for synchronous signal transmission between smartphone and STSHSP.
Excitation and emission of TPDCA. Inset: TPDCA under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right) (a). UV–vis absorption spectrum of TPDCA (b).

Emission spectra of TPDCA and TPDCA with berberine, inset: Optical images of TPDCA (left) and TPDCA+berberine (right) under UV light (a). Linearity of F/F0 and berberine concentration (Fluorimetry) (b). Voltage measured by STSHSP after adding different berberine concentrations (c). Linearity of V/V0 and berberine concentration by STSHSP (d).

UV–vis absorption spectra of TPDCA, berberine and TPDCA+berberine (a). UV–vis absorption spectra of berberine and fluorescence spectra of TPDCA (b). Fluorescence decay traces of TPDCA with different concentrations of berberine (c). Suppression rates (E, %) of observed (Eobsd) and corrected (Ecor) fluorescence intensity with the IFE removed from the Eobsd measurements of TPDCA after the addition of different berberine concentrations (d).
Schematic illustration of the preparation of synchronous transport signal hand-held sensing platform and its application in berberine sensor.
The results obtained by the STSHSP, fluorophotometer, and HPLC for different actual samples (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ND = Not determined. N/A = Not applicable.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The P-value was relative to the HPLC method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The unit of concentration was mg/mL.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: c
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The unit of concentration was mg/g.

head.ce:title: Development of an accurate synchronous transport signal hand-held sensing platform for fluorescence-based berberine on-site detection

Henan University of Animal Husbandry and Economy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Henan University of Animal Husbandry and Economy, Zhengzhou, Henan 450000, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Berberine is widely used in clinical treatment because of its wide antibacterial spectrum and low toxic side effects. However, its abuse could lead to bacterial resistance and several other adverse effects. In addition, measuring the content of berberine in environmental water samples helps to monitor its accumulation and metabolism in ecosystems. Traditional detection methods usually need to be carried out in the laboratory, involving complex processing procedures, which are not only time-consuming, but also unfavorable for rapid response and decision-making. Therefore, it is necessary to develop portable instruments to provide reasonable guidance on the addition and intake of berberine to reduce the harm caused by its abuse.

In this work, an accurate synchronous transport signal hand-held sensing platform (STSHSP) with a low-cost, easy-to-manufacture, independent use was developed by using photoelectric conversion technology, Bluetooth technology, remote synchronous signal technology, electrical technology, and 3D printing technology. To verify the performance of STSHSP, a 5-oxo-2,3-dihydro-5H-thiazolo [3,2-a] pyridine-3,7-dicarboxylic acid (TPDCA) with ultra-high quantum yield was designed and synthesized as a probe. TPDCA exhibited bright blue fluorescence under the ultraviolet light of 365 nm which could be quenched by berberine through the inner filter effect. In the range of 0.1–80 μg/mL, the voltage displayed by the prepared STSHSP has a good linearity with the berberine concentration (R2 = 0.9997) with a detection limit of 28.32 ng/mL. The portable sensor demonstrated good stability, accuracy, and reliability in detecting actual river water, urine, traditional Chinese medicine, and its preparation samples.

The sensor with its compact structure, portability, and simple operation was suitable for in-situ detection with accurate, reliable, and feasible results, which is beneficial for improving drug quality and ensuring human health. Fortunately, the device could transmit the information to the control center and/or a third-party supervision institution in real-time, which could effectively eliminate the trust crisis. The sensor has broad application prospects in the field of environmental water quality detection and drug safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have played a major role in preventing bacteria and treating infectious diseases in humans. However, the large and frequent use of antibiotics makes it difficult for them to be metabolized by humans or animals, leading to the entry into ecosystem and causing serious environmental pollution [1,2]. Among various antibiotics, berberine is a yellow crystalline powdered isoquinoline alkaloid (the chemical structure is shown in Fig. S1), which is widely present in many medicinal plants, including Coptis, Phellodendri, and other Chinese herbs [3]. Numerous studies have demonstrated that berberine exhibits positive pharmacological effects in many other diseases, such as cancer, reperfusion injury, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, HIV infection, and diabetes [4]. Yet, excessive use of berberine could lead to bacterial resistance and several other adverse effects, such as rash, liver damage, and hemolytic anemia [5]. Therefore, it is necessary to provide reasonable guidance on the addition and intake of berberine to ensure reasonable control and management of antibiotic residues in food and feed. In addition, measuring the content of berberine in environmental water samples helps to monitor its accumulation and metabolism in ecosystems. Several analytical methods for berberine detection have been reported, including high performance liquid chromatography [6], chemiluminescence [7], colorimetric assay [8], capillary electrophoresis [9], and electrochemistry determination [10]. Although these methods are accurate and efficient in certain situations, they are not portable enough to facilitate the development of field detection. As a result, it is crucial to develop a portable, precise method for the detection of berberine to strictly control the use of berberine.

Portable detection equipment is favored by analysts due to its advantages of small size, lightweight, low price, and its ability to meet the requirements of in-situ detection [11–13]. With the advent of laser, optical fiber, photon, and electronic devices, the performance and design flexibility of portable instruments based on absorption [14], infrared [15], and Raman spectroscopy [16] have steadily improved. Among various sensing devices, fluorescence is considered highly useful in practical applications for its high sensitivity, specificity, and fast response [17]. While traditional fluorescence instruments are indeed mature, they rely on intricate optical components and systems that result in high costs, bulkiness, and significant power consumption. Consequently, their integration into fully portable fluorescence sensing systems remains a significant challenge. In addition, there are some trust risks in the results of using portable testing equipment in the field far away from the laboratory and lacking effective supervision, which was usually effectively eliminated by introducing third-party supervision [18,19]. However, as far as we know, there was no report of portable fluorescence detection equipment with a remote synchronization function. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a portable fluorescence detection device that could transmit the detection data to the control center and/or the third-party supervision institution in real-time.

In this work, we designed and fabricated an accurate hand-held sensing platform with remote synchronous transport signal function (STSHSP) and achieved convenient, on-site, accurate, and low-cost fluorescence detection of berberine by using 5-oxo-2,3-dihydro-5H-thiazolo [3,2-a] pyridine-3,7-dicarboxylic acid (TPDCA) as a probe. The device was manufactured by 3D printing technology. While the analyte was present, the STSHSP converted the fluorescent signal emitted by the sample into an easy-to-detect electrical signal through photoelectric conversion technology, and the electrical signal was losslessly sent to the smartphone app through Bluetooth technology, which could be displayed and/or stored in the smartphone. The smartphone could remotely and synchronously transmit the detected data to the other smartphone app in a control center and/or a third-party supervision institution far away from the scene through Internet technology. As a result, it could enable the third-party to supervise the process of in-situ detection and eliminate the trust crisis (Scheme 1 ). Under optimal experimental conditions, we tested the potential of this STSHSP for berberine quantification with actual samples in both conditions and compared the results to those of the standard analysis methods.
All the reagents and apparatus used in this work were detailed in the Supplementary Information (SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of synchronous transport signal hand-held sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The main structure of STSHSP was designed and optimized by using the well-known 3D modeling software Rhino 6.0. Subsequently, STSHSP was manufactured by 3D printing technology using cheap polyethylene material doped with black dye. In order to improve the portability of STSHSP, the independent modular design was adopted, and the arrangement of accessories was gradually optimized to use the space more efficiently and reduce the size of the equipment to better adapt to the field detection far away from the laboratory. The optimal STSHSP was a cuboid with a size of 16.0 cm × 9.1 cm × 6.6 cm and a weight of 588.1 g, which was more suitable for in-situ detection than a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fig. 1 a and supporting movie 1-appearance and size of STSHSP.mp4).

Combining Fig. 1b and c, and supporting movie 2-Internal layout of STSHSP.mp4, the STSHSP was composed of two main components: fluorescence sensing and signal conversion and transmission. In the part of fluorescence sensing, a sample holder was used to fix a sample cell made of transparent quartz with a diameter of 10 mm, which was communicated with the surface of STSHSP for placing samples. The holder had a well-designed signal path and two vertical arms for installing the excitation source and photoelectric converter, so that the sample was excited by an LED with a narrow band of emission centered at 345 nm and power of 10 W to generate fluorescence and the photoelectric converter could receive the fluorescence signal from the sample. To remove the unnecessary stray light, there is a filter on each of the excitation and emission paths.

In the part of signal conversion and transmission, the photoelectric conversion device could convert the fluorescent signal generated by the sample into a voltage signal, which featured a large photosensitive area of 3.7 mm × 3.7 mm. These components were installed on the arm of the sample holder and perpendicular to the arm where the excitation source was installed. The data processing, display, and communication with smartphone functions of STSHSP were realized by the cooperation of a single-chip microcomputer (MCU), display, and Bluetooth. The LabView (National Instruments, Austin, Texas) was used to make the MCU have the functions of voltage data processing, voltage digital simulation, voltage display, and storage. The MCU was mainly used to process the voltage signal transmitted by the photoelectric converter and convert the signal into a displayable digital signal, which was displayed on a large screen with a size of 3.6 cm × 6.1 cm. The collected information could be delivered in real-time through Bluetooth communication on the microcontroller to the smartphone terminal connected with STSHSP, and then transmit the collected information to another smartphone terminal of the control center and/or a third-party supervision institution in real-time through the Internet.

In addition, the STSHSP also has some accessories. Aluminum-based radiator and radiator fan would help the device run stably. In order to meet the needs of in-situ detection, the power supply consisted of a large-capacity rechargeable battery, a constant voltage regulator, a single-pole double-throw switch, and two Type-C power sockets, which could provide two power supply modes for STSHSP including self-contained battery power supply and external 5 V power supply (such as power banks). The above components worked closely together to realize the in-situ detection function of STSHSP. Excitingly, the design method of STSHSP had good scalability and could be applied to the analysis of other fluorescence-based substances by replacing a few optoelectronic components (such as the excitation source), which was very tempting for fast and in-situ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of smartphone information collection and storage app
ce:section.ce:para: Android was the most widely used smartphone with over 85 % market share [20]. Therefore, the FLV-Test app for communication between smartphones and STSHSP was developed on the Android system to better record and save the measurement data of STSHSP. In the FLV-Test app, the smartphone collected the measurement data of STSHSP through the Bluetooth transmitter installed on the MCU. After installing the information collection app, the smartphone became an intelligent terminal for data collection and storage, which could display and store data accurately and conveniently. In this study, the smartphone app could share the collected measurement screens and data with other smartphones in the control center and/or the third-party supervision institution through the smartphone network, and it could also invite engineers to provide remote assistance when necessary. The designed operation interfaces of smartphone applications were shown in Fig. 2 . Fortunately, the design method of STSHSP had good adaptability and could be easy to install smartphone app to achieve data communication between STSHSP and any Android smartphone. As shown in Fig. S2 and supporting movie 3-operation method of STSHSP.mp4, STSHSP could remotely synchronize the detection signal to the control center and/or the third-party supervision institution through Bluetooth and Internet.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of verification probe for STSHSP performance
ce:section.ce:para: The simple, eco-friendly, and solvent-free synthetic procedure was used to synthesize TPDCA which was slightly modified from Shi and Dong's reported methods [21,22]. Namely, 8.400 g citric acid monohydrate and 4.844 g l-cysteine were ground into fine powder and then mixed for 20 min to obtain a well-dispersed mixture. The as-prepared solid mixture was placed in a beaker and reacted at 150 °C for 3 h in an oven and cooled down naturally. After the reaction, the white powdery solid mixture turned into a yellow viscous gel-like liquid and was evenly dispersed in 40 mL of ultrapure water. The yellow solid precipitate was separated by suction filtration and washed three times with ultrapure water to remove the impurities adsorbed on the surface and then dissolved in ultrapure water for recrystallization to improve purity. Finally, the yellow crystal was obtained.

According to the kinetic experiments of TPDCA and berberine (Fig. S3), the fluorescence intensity reached equilibrium nearly immediately and stabilized about 1 min after adding berberine, indicating that TPDCA could rapidly recognize berberine [23]. In order to ensure full quenching, the interaction time in the experiment was set at 5 min. The influence of pH, ion intensity, and matrix of pharmaceutical excipients of TPDCA were carried out according to the methods in SI. The interference experiments were also conducted by adding 50 μM of other ions or some common amino acids and determining in the same way [24].

The relationship between the concentration of quencher (berberine) and the fluorescence intensity of the substance (TPDCA) could be described by the Stern-Volmer equation (equation S(1)) at low concentrations [25]. So the concentration of berberine could be indirectly estimated by the fluorescence quenching degree of TPDCA. The fluorescence intensity of TPDCA would change after being quenched by berberine with different concentrations, which would change the electrical properties of the photoelectric converter for detecting fluorescence intensity. Therefore, the concentration of berberine in the sample could be calculated by the voltage displayed by STSHSP. To get the standard curve, TPDCA solution (7.5 μg/mL) and berberine solution (0.5 mg/mL) were used to prepare berberine standard solution with contents of 0, 0.03, 0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 5.0, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180 and 200 μg/mL. After mixing evenly, the fluorescence intensity was recorded by fluorometer and STSHSP which were used to calculate the regression equation.

Analysis of actual samples. The liquid samples of Weihe River water and urine were sampled following ISO 5667–6:2014 and GB/T 38735-2020 (China National Standard) for analysis, respectively [14]. The Chinese medicine and its preparation samples were processed according to the current edition of China Pharmacopoeia (2020 edition), and diluted or concentrated as required [26]. The details were presented in SI. The STSHSP was used to determine berberine in the samples, and the results were compared with those of high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC). The accuracy of the measurement results of the STSHSP was verified through one-way ANOVA results obtained by Excel software.

The obtained yellow crystal in section 2.4 was TPDCA, which was confirmed by 1H NMR, 13C NMR, HRMS, and single crystal X-ray diffractometer. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) of TPDCA (Fig. S4): δ 13.60 (s, 2H, COOH), 6.55–6.60 (d, 2H, CH), 5.48–5.46(d, H, CHCOOH), 3.59–3.94 (m, 2H, CH2). 13C NMR (100 MHz, DMSO‑d 6) of TPDCA (Fig. S5): δ 169.63, 166.08, 161.09, 150.42, 143.23, 115.52, 98.41, 63.08, 32.08. HRMS (ESI-TOF) m/z calculated for C9H7NO5S [M−H]−, 239.9961, found: 239.9962 (Fig. S6). According to Fig. S7 and Table S1, the single crystal X-ray results of TPDCA were consistent with NMR and HRMS.

The outstanding optical characteristics of TPDCA were the key to berberine sensing. TPDCA exhibited bright blue fluorescence under the ultraviolet light of 365 nm. The photoluminescence spectrum in Fig. 3 a revealed that TPDCA gives a strong emission peak at 416 nm under 346 nm excitation while FWHM was only 73 nm. The Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage 1931 chromaticity (CIE) color coordinates of TPDCA were (0.1535, 0.0656) in the blue light region (Fig. S8). The QY of TPDCA (aqueous solution) was as high as 80.65 % (Fig. S9), and the fluorescence lifetime was 9.7 ns. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum was employed to evaluate the optical properties of TPDCA. According to Fig. 3b, TPDCA had two absorption peaks at 244 nm and 346 nm. As shown in Fig.S10 and Fig.S11, TPDCA were low toxic and could be used for cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of berberine based on fluorophotometer
ce:section.ce:para: The pH would affect the ionization of the substance, thus affecting the optical properties of the fluorescent substance. There were carboxyl groups and tertiary amine groups that might be influenced by pH in the structure of TPDCA, so it is necessary to carry out the interference experiment of pH [27]. As shown in Fig. S12, the fluorescence intensity of TPDCA was not significantly affected by pH in the range of pH 4–10, and even at pH 3, the intensity was still above 86.3 %. Stability is an important parameter to evaluate the quality of probes [28,29]. As depicted in Fig. S13, the fluorescence intensity of TPDCA hardly changed after continuous exposure to 365 nm ultraviolet lamp for 3 h, and it remained over 85 % even after continuous exposure for 24 h. As shown in Fig. S14, the fluorescence intensity of TPDCA remained basically unchanged in the temperature range of 15 °C–40 °C, and it still remained above 90 % even at the temperature of 60 °C. Storage time was an important index to investigate the stability of the probe. Fig. S15 indicated that the fluorescence intensity of TPDCA remained unchanged even if it was stored under natural conditions for 14 days.

River water, urine, and traditional Chinese medicine often contain inorganic ions and amino acids [30–32]. Therefore, it is necessary to study the interference of common amino acids and inorganic ions. The fluorescence intensity of TPDCA wasn't affected by the addition of amino acids, and the minimum fluorescence intensity remained above 97.3 % (Fig. S16). The experimental results of ion intensity (Fig. S17) showed that the fluorescence intensity of the probe remained basically unchanged when the ion intensity was less than 400 mM. As shown in Fig. S18, berberine could significantly quench the fluorescence of TPDCA, while the inorganic ions didn't affect the fluorescence intensity of TPDCA, which also proved that TPDCA had good selectivity for berberine. Fig. S19 showed that the matrix interference of pharmaceutical excipients (1.0 mg/mL) commonly used in the preparation process on the determination of berberine by TPDCA was negligible. As shown in Fig. S21, TPDCA was insensitive to berberine analogues (1.0 mg/mL), which indicated that the probe had good selectivity. The matrix interference results of amino acids, ion intensity, inorganic ions, common pharmaceutical excipients, and berberine analogues in the actual samples indicated that TPDCA was an excellent berberine sensing probe with good application prospects.

In order to verify the sensing ability of berberine, the detection time, working concentration, and working temperature of TPDCA were explored. As depicted in Fig. S3, the fluorescence intensity achieved equilibrium almost instantaneously upon the addition of berberine and remained stable approximately 1 min later. As shown in Fig. S22, the sensing results of TPDCA for berberine remained stable across the temperature range of 15 °C–40 °C, indicating that it was appropriate and reasonable to use TPDCA for determining berberine at room temperature. According to Fig. S23, TPDCA had good sensitivity to berberine in the concentration range of 7.5–30 μg/mL [33,34]. Considering the above, in order to save the probe and facilitate the operation, a 7.5 μg/mL TPDCA was used to determine berberine concentration after incubation for 5 min at room temperature.

As shown in Fig. 4 a and Fig. S24, the TPDCA's fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with increasing berberine concentration. There was a negative correlation between the intensity and the value of berberine concentration from 0.03 to 200 μg/mL (Fig. 4b). The equation was F/F0 = −1.68 × 10−3X + 0.98951 (R2 = 0.9995), and the LOD was 2.09 ng/mL (See SI for details). The established fluorometer-based analytical method showed better linearity and lower detection limits relative to those reported in Table S2, indicating that it can be better utilized for analyzing berberine.

In order to analyze berberine content with STSHSP, an ingenious signal channel was established. The sample was excited by LED excitation light, which caused it to fluoresce. A photoelectric converter then transformed the fluorescence signal into an easy-to-detect voltage signal in the vertical direction, which was displayed on a screen for convenient reading and storage (Fig. 1b). In this case, the changes in the fluorescence intensity could be presented by the changes in the voltage signal and the berberine content could be obtained by measuring the voltage signal through the Stern-Volmer equation.

The analytical ability of STSHSP for berberine was evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4c and d, and supporting movie 3-operation method of STSHSP.mp4, the voltage displayed by STSHSP gradually decreased with increasing berberine concentration. In the range of 0.1–80 μg/mL, the voltage was linearly related to berberine concentration. The linearity could be described by the Stern-Volmer equation [35]. The regression equation was V/V0 = −7.51 × 10−3X + 0.99807 (R2 = 0.9997). The LOD was 28.32 ng/mL (See SI for details), which was similar to some reporting methods [36]. Compared with the methods reported in Table S2, the STSHSP could be employed to assay berberine, which had significant portability and price advantages compared with costly precision devices and had an excellent application prospect for on-site detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of berberine in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The recovery rate of actual samples measured by STSHSP was 97.10 %–100.18 %, as shown in Table S3, which was similar to that of 96.95 %–101.50 % by fluorometer. These results indicated that the STSHSP could be used for the efficient determination of berberine in water, urine, traditional Chinese medicine, and its preparations with TPDCA as a probe.

High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was recognized and widely used as an effective method for determination [37–39]. Referring to China Pharmacopoeia (2020 edition) and literatures [40–42], an HPLC method was established to determine berberine in actual samples (See SI for details), which exhibited good resolution and good peak shape (Fig. S25). In the range of 0.5–300 μg/mL, there was a perfect linearity between the peak area (y) and the berberine concentration (x). The linearity equation was y = 27.92169x – 0.5627 and the R2 = 0.9996 (Fig. S26).

The HPLC method was employed as a control. After water, urine, traditional Chinese medicine, and its preparations samples were pretreated in the same way, the berberine concentration was evaluated according to the standard operating procedures of the three apparatus (HPLC, fluorometer, and STSHSP), and the discrepancies between different determination methods were analyzed by one-way ANOVA. The results of actual sample detection and variance analysis were given in Table 1 . Compared with the HPLC method, there was no significant difference (P > 0.05), confirming the established STSHSP assay and fluorometer assay could afford reliable detection results in berberine determination.

In this work, a new type of organic fluorescent dye TPDCA probe was synthesized from cheap and easily available raw materials through a simple and green process. It was insensitive to pH, temperature, and ultraviolet rays, and could keep its fluorescence characteristics unchanged for a long time. In addition, TPDCA had the advantages of ultra-high quantum yield, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and high sensitivity, and could be used for real-time and nondestructive imaging.

The method had lower or similar detection limits, wider detection ranges, and higher accuracy of the regression formulas than other recently reported methods (Table S2). The determination of berberine by the method was basically unaffected by pH, temperature, ions, ionic intensity, amino acids, and pharmaceutical preparation matrix. Furthermore, the method had the advantages of high sensitivity, simple operation, high stability, fast analysis speed, wider application ranges and so on, and could achieve satisfactory detection performance. More importantly, the prepared STSHSP had high miniaturization, high portability, low preparation cost, easy operation, and the function of remote synchronization. This could not only solve the supervision and the crisis of trust in the detection on-site process, but also reduce the detection time and eliminate the limitation of large instruments by places and space. At the same time, the simple structure and low cost greatly improve manufacturing efficiency and consumer acceptance. Of course, there was still room for improvement in the method, such as the LOD, the equipment volume and appearance of STSHSP, and so on.

The quenching mechanism of fluorescent materials mainly included the inner filter effect (IFE), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), and static quenching effect (SQE) [43,44]. UV–vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectrum, and fluorescence lifetime were useful methods to reveal the quenching mechanism [45,46]. In order to precisely analyze the quenching mechanism of TPDCA fluorescence induced by berberine, the UV–vis spectra of TPDCA, berberine, and TPDCA+berberine were recorded [47]. According to Fig. 5 a, there was no new absorption peak in the UV–vis spectrum of TPDCA+berberine, so the quenching mechanism was not SQE [35,48]. The comparison of UV–vis and fluorescence spectra in Fig. 5b showed that the excitation and emission spectra of TPDCA almost completely overlapped with the absorption spectra of berberine at 346 nm and 416 nm, respectively, suggesting that the UV–vis spectrum of berberine could effectively shield the excitation and emission of TPDCA [49,50]. Furthermore, according to the table in Fig. 5c, the fluorescence lifetime of TPDCA remained basically unchanged after adding berberine with different concentrations, so the quenching mechanism was neither DQE nor FRET [51]. In summary, the quenching mechanism of berberine on the TPDCA's fluorescence was first considered to originate from IFE [49–51].

In order to investigate the mechanism of berberine quenching TPDCA fluorescence from the IFE, further experiments were conducted to explore the quenching mechanism of IFE. To eliminate the effects of the fluorescence cuvette's geometry (Fig. S27) and absorption properties, the results of TPDCA's aqueous solution spectrum were corrected according to equation S(4) [52–54]. The results were shown in Table S4. The IFE correction factor (CF) of berberine with each concentration was less than 3, which indicated that the correction was credible [53,55]. Additionally, the observed and corrected fluorescence efficiencies were calculated after different concentrations of berberine were added to TPDCA solutions (Fig. 5d). The results showed that IFE quenching efficiency was as high as 97.31 %, indicating that TPDCA fluorescence was quenched by berberine mainly via IFE [52–54].

In this work, a portable STSHSP was devised and produced with 3D printing, photoelectric conversion, short-distance wireless Bluetooth data transmission, and long-distance network transmission technologies. In order to achieve high sensitivity detection, a blue fluorescent TPDCA with good stability, low toxicity, and high quantum yield (80.65 %) was chosen as the probe. Berberine could be accurately sensed by TPDCA through the IFE effect, for which the analytical method was developed. The as-prepared STSHSP could be well-used for the berberine detection with the linearity equation of V/V0 = −7.51 × 10−3X + 0.99807 (R2 = 0.9997) in the range of 0.1–80 μg/mL and the LOD of 28.32 ng/mL. The sensor's accuracy, reliability, and feasibility were verified in the detection of berberine in actual water, urine, traditional Chinese medicine, and its preparation samples, and the results obtained by STSHSP were identical to those obtained by HPLC (P > 0.05). The device with its compact structure, portability, simple operation, and to synchronize data remotely was suitable for in-situ detection. In addition, the device could transmit the collected information to another smartphone terminal of the control center and/or a third-party supervision institution in real-time through the Internet, which could effectively eliminate the trust crisis. The development of this sensor has broad application prospects in the field of environmental water quality detection and drug safety.

Liucheng Guo: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Liyue Du: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Yan Zhang: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jie Gao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Fengling Cui: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
